title: Dio
objectives: Use interceptors for auth, logging, retries

sections.markdown: Dio is the de-facto HTTP client for Flutter — powerful base options, interceptors, form data, file up/download with progress, and CancelTokens. Ideal when you need more than the standard `http` package.

The interceptor chain is Dio's superpower: attach the token, refresh on 401, log requests, retry with backoff, and adapt responses — all without touching call sites. Order matters: auth before retry, logging outermost.
Sharing one Dio globally without CancelTokens. When the user leaves a screen, in-flight requests keep running (and setState-after-dispose). Pass a CancelToken per screen and cancel on dispose().
Set baseUrl + timeouts once. Map DioException.type to a typed AppError. Use `receiveDataWhenStatusError: true` to read error bodies. For gzip/brotli, use dio_smart_retry or a custom Adapter.

flashcards.back: Interceptors (auth, retry, logging), CancelTokens, upload/download progress, form data, custom adapters.
Attach a CancelToken to each call; token.cancel() cancels them.
Log → Auth → Retry (or Auth → Retry → Log outermost) — auth attaches token before retry considers a failure.

quiz.q: Which is NOT built into Dio?
quiz.explain: Dio provides HTTP primitives; OAuth flow is your responsibility.

quiz.q: To cancel requests on screen dispose…
Handling a 401 across all endpoints is best done via…
options: An interceptor that refreshes the token

challenges.text: Configure a Dio instance with baseUrl + timeouts + a LogInterceptor.
Add an AuthInterceptor that refreshes a token on 401.
Add exponential backoff for 5xx + connection errors.
Wrap Dio in a typed API client, mapping DioException → AppError variants.

interviewQuestions: How to handle 401 with token refresh?
How to test code that uses Dio?
